### Implementing generative AI with speed and safety

### Gen AI opportunities

* potential for transformative impact across innovation, growth, and productivity  
* Can produce credible software, code, text, hi-fi images, videos  
* To add $4.4T in economic value to the global economy  
* Organizations looking to deploy **high-potential use cases for gen AI** to drive productivity and innovation; provide better, more consistent customer service; and boost creativity in marketing and sales

### Organisation appetite for Gen AI

* In one research 53% of $50Mn annual revenue organisations rated Gen AI as ‘high priority’   
* 91% of these orgs stated that there were not prepared to initiate Gen AI use in a responsible way   
* 

### Gen AI risks 

* Inaccurate outputs  
* Biases embedded in the underlying training data  
* Potential for large scale misinformation   
* malicious influence on politics and personal well-being  
* Inability to manage the novelty and complexity of issues  
* **Inbound threats and Gen AI initiative threats**  \- organisation will face some risks to itself even when it doesn’t implement its own AI capability   
* **Risk of Leaning towards low risk usecases only** \- Missteps and failures can erode the confidence of executives, employees, and customers and trigger scaling back in the level of ambition to ultrasafe use cases that generate limited risk   
* **Risks of being unlikely to capitalize on the technology’s true potential** with extra cautious approach 

### How to implement Gen AI with speed and safety

* By adapting proven risk management approaches to Gen AI  
* Building fit for purpose risk management to guard against threats 

### Target state for orgs 

* Capture Gen AI value at pace and responsibly   
* Operate effectively while the regulatory environment around AI continues to evolve

### Organisations GEN AI aspirations 

* **Maker** \- it could be looking to be a maker of the foundation models,   
* **Shaper** \- a shaper that customizes and scales foundation models,   
* **Taker** \- or a taker that adopts foundation models through off-the-shelf applications with little or no customization

### How to prepare for Inbound threats to orgs in fraud and cyber domains

* Launch a sprint to **understand** the risk of inbound exposures related to gen AI.  
* Develop a comprehensive view of the materiality of gen-AI-related risks across domains and use cases, and build a range of options (including both technical and nontechnical measures) to manage risks.  
* Establish a governance structure that balances expertise and oversight with an ability to support rapid decision making, adapting existing structures whenever possible.  
* Embed the governance structure in an operating model that draws on expertise across the organization and includes appropriate training for end users.  
* Factors to refine the approach to the specific organisation  \- Organisation’s own Gen AI aspirations

#### Step 1: Understanding inbound risks types

Mckinsey classifies all risks from Gen AI \- whether or not the org has deployed Gen AI in the following 8 categories 

* **Impaired fairness \-**  algorithm bias from unrepresentative training data or model   
* **Intellectual Property (IP) infringement**   
* **Data privacy and quality** \- unauthorized use or disclosure of sensitive info   
* **Malicious use \-** harmful AI generate content \- falsehoods / deepfakes of company executives / scams / hate speech   
* **Security threats \-** vulnerabilities in Gen AI systems \- payload splitting to bypass safety filters, manipulating open source models, gen-AI enabled malware   
* **Performance and explainability \-** Inability to explain model outputs or model inaccuracies \- factually incorrect or outdated answers, hallucinations  
* **Strategic risks \-** noncompliance  with standards and regulations, societal risks, reputational risks   
* **Third party risks** \- risks associated with use of 3P AI tools \- proprietary data used by public models 

##### **Handling Inbound risks** 

**Step 1 A focused sprint to understand** 

* **Understand potential exposures to inbound risks** anchored in the org’s risk profile   
* **Understand How many third parties have access to sensitive data** that need to be restricted from training external gen AI models   
* **Understand maturity and readiness of the control environment** \- ie, it’s tech and non tech capabilities to prevent, detect and respond to inbound risks ie what cyber and fraud defenses it has   
* **Understand ability to limit the scraping of company IP by engines** used to train large language models.

**Step 2** 

* Map out areas of max exposure   
* Where do current defense systems stand   
* Create roadmap of where the org should harden defenses   
* Identify potential ROI from these risk mitigation efforts   
* Deploy the defense systems and practices  
* Repeat process to review the vulnerabilities regularly 

![][image1]

##### **Handling risks from when the org itself is undergoing Gen AI adoption** 

* Additional ongoing efforts are needed here   
* Will require investment of time and resources and shift in the ways of working   
* Gen AI adoption use cases again fall under the same **8 categories** as those for Inbound Risks, only that the use cases have varying risk profiles,   
  * reflecting both the nature of the technology itself   
  * and company-specific context concerning the specifics of the use case  
  * Ofcourse since the org itself is implementing the usecase, there is no 3P risk area in a way  

#### Step 1 Calibrate risks

* it’s important to develop a rubric to calibrate expectations of what constitutes a high versus a medium risk across categories \- otherwise orgs can run into disagreements   
* Now I have another document just on Risk tiers based on the EU AI act \- [AI - Risk Tiers in AI - EU AI Act](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1frqhT-Ee90T2KWlvrb8NpuxRTiGxwrmzlVKTfEbxjSc/edit?tab=t.0)  
* It’s essential that the executive in charge of the use case leads the initial assessment of the risks associated with it (**as part of the role of the product manager** in an effective operating model).  
* Set up Governance group a cross-functional group, including business heads and members of legal and compliance functions, should review and validate the risk assessments for all use cases

#### Step 2 Risk profiling 

* map the potential risks associated with each case across key risk categories to assess the potential risk severity

Example of use case risk profiling \- Low / Med / High risk across the 8 risk categories 

**Step 3 \- Consider options to manage risks**   
**Now the org should develop strategies to manage risks by mitigation and robust governance** 

* Many (but not all) mitigations are technical in nature and can be implemented across the life cycle of the process.  
* these controls don’t all need to be embedded in the underlying foundation model itself (which many organizations won’t have access to). Some can be overlays built in the local environment, as is the case of a gen-AI-enabled chatbot designed by an HR department to field employee queries about benefits

![][image2]  
![][image3]  
![][image4]  
In this use case

* The possible **technical mitigations** are   
  * having the chatbot **ask clarifying questions** to generate additional necessary user inputs  
  * having the **user confirm that the chatbot has properly understood the query**  
  * **limiting the types of data sets that the chatbot can access** (for example, excluding personal information)  
  * **designing the chatbot to provide citations** to explain its answers and allow for fact-checking of its responses  
  * take steps (such as limiting repeated interactions) to f**rustrate the attack vectors** and jailbreaking that are known to create challenges for chatbots  
  * develop **classifiers to identify and reject out-of-scope queries** (such as requesting calculations).  
* **Non Technical mitigations**  
  * maintaining **humans in the loop** to guard against the technology being able to put outputs directly into production  
  * contractual p**rovisions to guard against problematic use of data from third parties**  
  * **Extend mitigations to many use cases \-** get scaled benefits from their technical mitigations rather than having to create bespoke approaches for each case \- example \- providing **citations to address challenges of explainability** can be applied to all use cases

### Balancing speed to scale with judicious risk management through governance

* Using Gen AI will need the orgs to adapt its governance structures to respond to demand on approvals and to exercise oversight   
* We don’t need entirely new committees \- we just need to adapt the existing ones   
* Avoid setting up new committees as they might cause disruption, add friction to decision making and confusion over accountability.

![][image5]  
**Orgs need to** 

1. **Create a cross-functional, responsible gen AI steering group with at least a monthly cadence**  
   1. Include business, tech, data, privacy, legal and compliance members   
   2. Should have a mandate for making critical decisions   
   3. Should review foundational strategy decisions \- such as the selection of foundational models and compatibility with the organization’s risk posture  
   4. a single individual empowered to handle coordination and agenda setting \- e.g. an AI governance officer   
2. **Create Responsible AI guidelines and policies \-**    
   1. Agreed with the executive team and board members  
   2. To serve as a guardrail for acceptable AI usecases  
   3. Set principles on the extent of genAI that can be used for use case types \-e.g. Stance on  the use of gen AI to support employment decisions (including hiring and performance reviews)  
   4. the conditions under which gen AI outputs can be put directly into production without human review  
   5. Refresh existing policies for genAI development and use (for example, covering misrepresentation and IP infringement).  
3. **Create Responsible AI talent and culture**   
   1. Cascade responsible AI culture across the org   
   2. accountability, capability building, and awareness tailored to the relevant degree of exposure  
   3. organization-wide training on responsible AI should be developed and rolled out to foment a broad understanding of the dynamics of inbound risk and how to engage with the technology safely  
   4. Develop deep understanding of ethics and “responsibility by design” to embed risk considerations early in the design and engineering processes.  
4. Embed responsible AI into 4 types of roles \- There are **four critical roles required for successful implementation** of responsible AI  throughout the use cases, where the responsibilities of these roles are tied closely to their talent and expected actions in pushing forward use cases  
   1. **Designer / Product Managers**  
      1. **steer the direction of gen AI deployment** by identifying new use cases with an awareness of how they fit into the organization’s overall gen AI strategy and road map  
      2. Are **accountable for identifying and mitigating relevant risks**  
      3. Have an important role in **driving the cultural changes required to adopt gen AI**  
      4. Accountable to **build trust in the proposition** that business value can be achieved responsibly and safely for employees and customers.  
           
   2. **Engineers**  \- Are technical experts who understand mechanics of GenAI  
      1. develop or customize the technology to support the gen AI use cases  
      2. **responsible** for guiding on the technical feasibility of mitigations  
      3. Accountable for coding the mitigations to limit risk, as well as developing technical-monitoring strategies.  
           
   3. **Governors** \- Governors make up the teams that help establish the necessary governance, processes, and capabilities to drive responsible and safe implementation practices for gen AI  
      1. establish the core risk frameworks, guardrails, and principles to guide the work of designers and engineers  
      2. challenging risk evaluation and mitigation effectiveness (especially for higher-risk use cases)  
      3. AI Governance Office or similar roles will ideally cover data risk, data privacy, cybersecurity, regulatory compliance, and technology risk.  
           
   4. Users \- represent the end users of new gen AI tools or use cases  
      1. need to be trained and acculturated to the dynamics and potential risks of the technology  
      2. play a critical role in helping identify risks from gen AI use cases, as they may experience problematic outputs in their interactions with the model.

The org operating model should account for how the different personas will interact at different stages of the gen AI life cycle.

Sources: 

1. [https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/risk-and-resilience/our-insights/implementing-generative-ai-with-speed-and-safety](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/risk-and-resilience/our-insights/implementing-generative-ai-with-speed-and-safety)
